claim: The actual chemical composition of the investigated Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C steel is Fe-0.15C-7.35Mn-6.00Al (wt%) with P<0.005 and S=0.01.
evidence_text: Table 1 Chemical composition of the investigated alloys (wt%). C 0.15 Mn 7.35 Al 6.00 P < 0.005 S 0.01 Fe Bal.

claim: After hot forging, the ingot was homogenized at 1200 °C for 2 hours before hot rolling.
evidence_text: after being homogenized at 1200 °C for 2 h, the ingot was hot rolled

claim: The homogenized ingot was hot rolled to 3 mm thickness sheet in 6 passes, with start rolling temperature of 1150 °C and finish rolling temperature of 850 °C, then water quenched to 550 °C and air cooled to room temperature.
evidence_text: hot rolled to 3 mm thickness steel sheet through 6 passes. The start rolling and finish rolling temperatures were 1150 °C and 850 °C, respectively. The hot-rolled steel was subsequently water quenched to 550 °C, followed by air cooling to room temperature.

claim: Solution treatments were performed at 700, 800, 900, and 1000 °C for 1 hour each, followed by water quenching; the corresponding specimens are designated H70, H80, H90, and H100, respectively.
evidence_text: solution treatment experiments were performed at 700–1000 °C for 1 h with a water quenching followed. For convenience, the specimens being solution treated at 700, 800, 900 and 1000 °C are referred to as H70, H80, H90 and H100, respectively.

claim: After solution treatment at 900 °C for 1 h, the H90 sample exhibits a duplex microstructure consisting of ferrite and approximately 30% austenite.
evidence_text: after being solution treated at 900 °C for 1 h, the steel has a duplex microstructure consisting of ferrite and ~ 30% austenite

claim: Austenite volume fraction was determined by XRD with Cu Kα radiation using the direct comparison method, in which (200)α and (211)α peaks represent ferrite and (220)γ and (311)γ peaks represent austenite.
evidence_text: The volume fraction of austenite was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation, and the direct comparison method was adopted, in which the integrated intensities of the peaks of (200)α and (211)α are recognized as ferrite, and those of the peaks of (220)γ and (311)γ are regarded as austenite.

claim: The carbon content in austenite was determined from the austenite lattice parameter using the equation αγ = 3.578 + 0.0330 X_C + 0.0056 X_Al + 0.00095 X_Mn (concentrations in wt%).
Austenite lattice parameter αγ is a function of C, Mn, Al concentrations (in wt%) and was determined from the d-spacing of (220)γ.
evidence_text: where α_γ is austenite lattice parameter, in Å, and X_C, X_Mn and X_Al are concentrations of C, Mn and Al, respectively, in wt%. The austenite lattice parameter (α_γ) was determined from a d-spacing of (220)γ position.

claim: In H70 (solution treated at 700 °C for 1 h), nearly all original austenite decomposed to martensite and spheroidal M5C2 carbide, with a retained austenite volume fraction of 12.2%.
evidence_text: For H70 (shown in Fig. 1a), nearly all the original austenite phase decomposed to martensite and spheroidal M5C2-carbide, with a volume fraction of 12.2% being retained.

claim: M5C2-carbide phase widely distributed both at δ-ferrite grain boundaries and among martensite laths in H70.
evidence_text: M5C2-carbide phase widely distributed both at δ-ferrite grain boundaries and among martensite laths.

claim: In H80 (800 °C, 1 h), M5C2-carbide disappeared, martensite reverse-transformed to austenite, and a large amount of strip α-ferrite was observed.
evidence_text: For H80 (shown in Fig. 1b), M5C2-carbide disappeared, martensite has reverse-transformed to austenite. Large amount of strip ferrite, which is termed as α-ferrite in order to distinguish it from δ-ferrite that is formed at high temperature, was observed.

claim: In H90 (900 °C, 1 h), the microstructure mainly consisted of α-ferrite, δ-ferrite and austenite; α-ferrite morphology changed from strip shape to fine blocky shape and the austenite volume fraction increased to 33.8%.
evidence_text: For H90, with the solution temperature increasing to 900 °C, the microstructure of specimen was mainly consisted of α-ferrite, δ-ferrite and austenite, as shown in Fig. 1c. Besides, the morphology of α-ferrite changed from strip shape into fine blocky shape and the volume fraction of austenite increased to 33.8%.

claim: Phases were distinguished by Vickers microhardness: austenite 275±10 HV, δ-ferrite 314±15 HV, α-ferrite 374±10 HV, and martensite 438±15 HV.
evidence_text: Phase Vickers microhardness, HV Austenite 275 ± 10 δ-ferrite 314 ± 15 α-ferrite 374 ± 10 Martensite 438 ± 15

claim: M5C2-carbide was identified according to EDS.
evidence_text: And M5C2-carbide was identified according to EDS.

claim: H70 exhibited yield strength of 642.1 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 778.3 MPa, total elongation of 23.0%, and austenite volume fraction of 12.2%.
H80 exhibited yield strength of 587.8 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 724.5 MPa, total elongation of 30.5%, and austenite volume fraction of 20.7%.
H90 exhibited yield strength of 473.6 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 846.4 MPa, total elongation of 32.0%, and austenite volume fraction of 33.8%.
H100 exhibited yield strength of 398.3 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 860.3 MPa, total elongation of 15.0%, and austenite volume fraction of 12.9%.
For H90, true stress increased abruptly at a true strain of ~0.03, and both UTS and TE were the highest among the four steels.
evidence_text: for H90, true stress increased abruptly at a true strain of ~0.03, and both the UTS and TE were the highest among the four steels.

claim: The steels possessed an obvious three-stage work hardening: stage 1 WH rate decreased rapidly (ferrite deformation), stage 2 slight decrease (strong hardening mechanism e.g. TRIP or TWIP effect), stage 3 WH rate decreased quickly again (deformation localization of ferrite or martensite).
evidence_text: As shown in Fig. 3b, the steels possesses an obvious threestage work hardening (WH) characteristics: in stage 1, WH rate decreased rapidly, which was mainly associated with the deformation of ferrite; In stage 2, the slight decrease of the WH rate is a consequence of the strong hardening mechanism, such as TRIP or TWIP effect; In stage 3, the WH rate decreased quickly again, and this was presumably associated with the deformation localization of ferrite or martensite.

claim: At true strain 0.20, over 60% of retained austenite had Schmid factors smaller than 0.40, with an average Schmid factor less than 0.34.
evidence_text: With true strain increased to 0.20, over 60% retained austenite had Schmid factors smaller than 0.40, the average Schmid factor of the retained austenite is less than 0.34.

claim: Table 4 reports the elemental fractions and Ms/Md30 temperatures: H70 has C 0.556 mass%, Mn 8.91 mass%, Al 5.22 mass%, Ms 33°C, Md30 168°C; H80 has C 0.479 mass%, Mn 8.85 mass%, Al 5.1 mass%, Ms 67°C, Md30 204°C; H90 has C 0.391 mass%, Mn 8.34 mass%, Al 4.67 mass%, Ms 120°C, Md30 249°C.
evidence_text: Table 4 The elements fraction and Ms, Md30 temperature. H70: C 0.556 mass%, Mn 8.91 mass%, Al 5.22 mass%, Ms 33 °C, Md30 168 °C; H80: C 0.479 mass%, Mn 8.85 mass%, Al 5.1 mass%, Ms 67 °C, Md30 204 °C; H90: C 0.391 mass%, Mn 8.34 mass%, Al 4.67 mass%, Ms 120 °C, Md30 249 °C.

claim: With increasing tensile strain, blocky austenite takes the initiative to transform into martensite due to higher localized stress at grain boundaries and phase boundaries, where a triaxial state of stress and more strain are favored.
evidence_text: With the increase of tensile strain, the blocky austenite took the initiative to transform into martensite, which can be attributed to the higher localized stress. ... Consequently, a triaxial state of stress and more strain can be favored on these blocky austenite grains during deformation.

claim: As deformation continues, dislocation multiplication in martensite and wavy glide in ferrite lead to complicated dislocation structures, restricting dynamic recovery and dislocation climbing, thereby enhancing strength performance.
evidence_text: As the deformation continues, dislocation multiplied constantly in martensite, while wavy glide in ferrite leading to the formation of complicated dislocation structure. ... Thus, the dynamic recovery and dislocation climbing were restricted, and the strength performance were enhanced eventually.

claim: The calculated Ms and Md30 temperatures for H90 are 120°C and 249°C, for H80 67°C and 204°C, and for H70 33°C and 268°C, with the higher Ms in H90 attributed to lower C and Mn contents; lower C content enables a metastable austenite state to induce martensite transformation.
evidence_text: H90 were 120 °C and 249 °C, H80 were 67 °C and 204 °C, and H70 were 33 °C and 268 °C, respectively. Due to the lower C and Mn contents, the corresponding temperature in H90 was much higher than that in other cases. It can be concluded that C content plays a significant role in austenite stability, and that lower C content enables austenite a metastable state to induce martensite transformation in this paper.

claim: XRD analysis shows that after tensile deformation, the austenite volume fraction decreased in all steels; the martensite transformation ratio increased with solution temperature from 700 to 900°C, reaching a peak of 89.9% in H90, indicating enhanced TRIP effect.
evidence_text: Fig. 7. (a) The XRD profiles and (b) the corresponding austenite fractions for the steels before and after tensile deformation. ... In all steels, the austenite volume fraction decreased after tensile tests. As the solution temperature increasing in the range 700-900 °C, the martensite transformation ratio increased gradually, with a peak value of 89.9% in H90. This also suggests an enhanced TRIP effects in association with a metastable stability of austenite.

claim: The hot-rolled Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C steel solution treated at 900°C for 1 h, with a dual-phase ferrite and over 30% austenite microstructure, exhibited the best combination of mechanical properties: ultimate tensile strength of 836.4 MPa, yield strength of 473.6 MPa, and total elongation of 32%.
evidence_text: The hot-rolled steel solution treated at 900 °C for 1 h, possessing a dual-phase microstructure consisting of ferrite and over 30% austenite, exhibited best combination of mechanical properties, and was characterized by excellent combination of UTS of 836.4 MPa, YS of 473.6 MPa, and TE of 32%.

claim: Because of the variation in element partitioning, the martensitic transformation ratio increased with the solution temperature in the range 700–900°C, and the Ms temperature was another factor influencing austenite stability.
evidence_text: Because of the variation in element partitioning, the martensitic transformation ratio increased with the solution temperature in the range 700-900 °C. Moreover, M_s temperature was another factor influencing the austenite stability.

claim: The steel exhibited three-stage work hardening, with stage 2 linked to the TRIP effect; austenite grains at grain boundaries between multiple ferrite grains transformed first, while those in ferrite laths or embedded in larger ferrite grains rotated; the small-angle grain boundary fraction increased greatly to over 51% at true strain 0.27.
evidence_text: the steel exhibited an obvious three-stage work hardening characteristics, in which the S2 was linked to the TRIP effect. ... The austenite grains positioned at grain boundaries between multiple ferrite grains took the initiative to transform, whereas some other austenite grains, which were located in ferrite laths or embedded in larger ferrite grains, were often found to undergo rotations. In addition, the volume fraction of small angle grain boundaries increased greatly, with a value of over 51% being observed at the true strain of 0.27.

claim: The hot-rolled Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C steel solution treated at 900 °C for 1 h, possessing a dual-phase microstructure consisting of ferrite and over 30% austenite, exhibited the best combination of mechanical properties with UTS of 836.4 MPa, YS of 473.6 MPa, and TE of 32%.
evidence_text: The hot-rolled steel solution treated at 900 °C for 1 h, possessing a dual-phase microstructure consisting of ferrite and over 30% austenite, exhibited best combination of mechanical properties, and was characterized by excellent combination of UTS of 836.4 MPa, YS of 473.6 MPa, and TE of 32%.

claim: Because of the variation in element partitioning, the martensitic transformation ratio increased with the solution temperature in the range 700–900 °C.
evidence_text: Because of the variation in element partitioning, the martensitic transformation ratio increased with the solution temperature in the range 700–900 °C.

claim: The M_s temperature was another factor influencing the austenite stability.
evidence_text: Moreover, M_s temperature was another factor influencing the austenite stability.
